metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten the AC 4 Dockerfile grep to anchor on the URL-scoped config key form, e.g. `grep -q 'credential\.' infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot` (or better, `grep -qE 'credential\.[^.]+\.helper' infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot`), so the command only passes if the URL-scoped helper key is present.
findings.finding: The AC 4 infra proving command uses `grep -q 'credential'` to verify the URL-scoped git credential helper, but this pattern is too broad — it matches any line containing the word 'credential' (comments, other helpers, documentation) and does NOT verify that the correct URL-scoped config key form (`credential.<tickets-url>.helper`) is actually present. The AC claim (URL-scoped, not global) is therefore not proved by this command.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC 4 proving command: `grep -q 'credential' infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `grep -q 'credential' infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot` with `grep -qE 'credential\.[^.]+\.helper' infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot` in the AC 4 proving command.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Describe how `changed_files` and `diff_text` are obtained in voter.py at the emit point (e.g. passed up from `adapter.code_review_decision`, extracted from the Gerrit client already in scope, or fetched via a new call) so the `change_fingerprint` inputs are fully specified.
findings.finding: Section B specifies `change_fingerprint(change_id, revision, changed_files, diff_text)` as the function voter.py must compute and pass to the sidecar, but Section C's description of the voter.py emit point only confirms that `decision`, `commit_message`, `change_id`, and `revision` are in scope — `changed_files` and `diff_text` are never described as available at that call site. The plan does not describe how voter.py obtains these two required inputs.
findings.location: Section B ('change_fingerprint' definition) vs Section C ('In `review_and_vote`, AFTER the successful `store.record_vote`...')
findings.suggested_fix: In Section C step 2 (or the adapter threading description), explicitly state that `changed_files` and `diff_text` are threaded up via `adapter.code_review_decision`'s return dict alongside `verdict` (mirroring how `verdict` is threaded), and add both fields to the list of in-scope variables at the voter.py emit point.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify or refute the 'single background worker serializes reviews' claim against voter.py's actual concurrency model (per-(change_id,revision) asyncio locks), and add an explicit global worktree-level serialization mechanism (e.g., a single asyncio.Lock or a file lock) if concurrent reviews of different changes can race on the shared tickets worktree.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the reviewbot's single background worker serializes reviews, so the shared worktree needs no extra lock.' But voter.py uses per-(change_id, revision) asyncio locks — NOT a global serializer — so concurrent reviews of DIFFERENT changes run simultaneously via `asyncio.to_thread`. Multiple concurrent reviews emitting to the same tickets worktree git repository will race on `git commit` / `git push`, which are not atomic under concurrent access.
findings.location: Section D (Infra) — 'Concurrency: the reviewbot's single background worker serializes reviews, so the shared worktree needs no extra lock.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a module-level `_tickets_worktree_lock = asyncio.Lock()` in voter.py (or in the new artifact-emit helper) and `async with _tickets_worktree_lock:` around the create/emit/link/push sequence, ensuring only one review at a time touches the shared worktree.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the `registry.py` entry in the plan: the `suppress_types` exemption is data-driven (from criterion definition JSON/YAML), so the actual edits are in the criteria data files (e.g. `criteria_routing.json` or YAML criterion defs), not in `registry.py` Python source.
findings.finding: The plan names `llm/plan_review/registry.py` as a guard site for the `('bug','session_log')` short-circuit, but the relevant code in `registry.py` is the `suppress_types` field in criterion definitions (`applies_at.suppress_types`), not a hardcoded `('bug','session_log')` tuple in Python source. The `applies_at_criterion` function at line 333 checks `ticket_type in (ap.get('suppress_types') or [])` — this is data-driven from the criteria JSON. Adding `code_review` here requires updating the criterion JSON files (`criteria_routing.json` or the YAML criteria), NOT a Python tuple. This is a different kind of edit than the plan implies.
findings.location: Section A ('Gate exemption'); `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/registry.py:329-333`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `registry.py` in the exemption-axis list with the specific criteria data files that contain `suppress_types: [bug, session_log]` entries, or annotate it as 'no Python edit; update suppress_types in the criteria JSON/YAML definition files'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how 'all commits in the change' trailer extraction works for merge patchsets: either (a) iterate the CommitInfo list already returned by gc.get_mergelist() (each entry has a 'message' field) and union refs from those messages alongside the top-level commit_message, or (b) document that only the merge commit's own message is checked and update the 'all commits' claim accordingly.
findings.finding: The 'all commits' trailer-resolution mechanism is incomplete for merge patchsets: the plan says to call extract_ticket_refs(commit_message) over 'ALL commits in the change (parse each commit's message, union the refs)', but commit_message in voter.py holds only the top-level commit message from gc.get_commit(). For merge patchsets, ticket trailers may live in the messages of integrated commits returned by gc.get_mergelist() — each CommitInfo in that list carries a 'message' field — but the plan names no step to iterate those messages. A merge-path patchset whose trailer refs are in integrated-commit messages will produce zero links despite valid rebar-ticket trailers.
findings.location: Section C — 'TRAILER RESOLUTION: verify_commit.extract_ticket_refs(commit_message) over ALL commits in the change'
findings.suggested_fix: In section C, replace 'over ALL commits in the change (parse each commit's message, union the refs)' with explicit steps: on the non-merge path use commit_message directly; on the merge path union refs from commit_message AND from each CommitInfo['message'] in the mergelist already returned by _assemble_merge_diff (no extra REST call needed — the data is already in scope).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify what the sidecar emit does when decision came from _merge_coverage_gap_decision() (no adapter verdict available): either skip emit for coverage-gap decisions, or emit a minimal synthetic payload marking the record as a coverage_gap artifact with decision='BLOCK' and verdict=None explicitly documented as valid.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify what is emitted in the artifact sidecar when the review decision was produced by _merge_coverage_gap_decision() rather than by adapter.code_review_decision(). The plan says 'the FULL verdict is threaded up from adapter.code_review_decision (a NEW verdict key ADDED to its return dict)' and the sidecar emit uses that key — but _merge_coverage_gap_decision() bypasses the adapter entirely and produces {decision, message, findings, coverage_gap} with no 'verdict' key. The emit path would receive verdict=None for these cases.
findings.location: Section C step 2 — 'The FULL verdict is threaded up from adapter.code_review_decision (a NEW verdict key ADDED to its return dict)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the concurrency claim in Section D: the reviewbot reviews different (change_id, revision) pairs concurrently (per-key asyncio.Lock, not a global serializer), so the shared tickets-worktree artifact emit path requires either (a) a global asyncio.Lock around the create+commit+push sequence or (b) a per-worktree file lock (e.g., the existing `rebar._store.lock`) to serialize git operations.
findings.finding: The plan claims the shared tickets-worktree 'needs no extra lock' because 'the reviewbot's single background worker serializes reviews,' but the actual voter.py uses per-(change_id, revision) asyncio.Lock objects, meaning reviews of DIFFERENT (change_id, revision) pairs run concurrently via asyncio.to_thread. The artifact create + link + push for two concurrent reviews would race on the shared git worktree (git operations are not asyncio-aware), potentially corrupting the worktree or producing duplicate artifacts.
findings.location: Section D (Infra) — 'Concurrency: the reviewbot's single background worker serializes reviews, so the shared worktree needs no extra lock'; voter.py lines 43-58 (_locks dict, per-key asyncio.Lock)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete return-shape contract for all `adapter.code_review_decision` branches (PASS, BLOCK-finding, all coverage-gap sub-reasons, error/exception fallbacks) specifying whether each carries a non-None `verdict` key and what the voter should do when `verdict` is absent or None before calling `sidecar.emit`.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `adapter.code_review_decision`'s return dict gains a new `'verdict'` key carrying the full four-pass verdict, but provides no protocol for how the voter distinguishes a new-shape response (with `verdict`) from an error/fallback response (which returns `_block(...)` — a dict with no `verdict` key). The current adapter always returns `{decision, message, findings, coverage_gap}` and voter.py reads only `decision` and `message` (lines 463-465). The plan says 'the voter does NOT re-invoke produce_code_review_verdict' — but the sidecar emit requires the full verdict with `blocking[]`, `advisory[]`, `coverage{}` etc., none of which are in the current return dict.
findings.location: Section C ('Reviewbot emit'), step 2; review_bot/adapter.py; review_bot/voter.py lines 463-465

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `tickets_branch` and `tickets_remote` are made resolvable from the freestanding `/var/gerrit/site/reviewbot-tickets` worktree — e.g. by placing a `rebar.toml` with `[tracker] branch = "tickets"` and `[sync] remote = "origin"` at `/var/gerrit/site/` (the parent), or by configuring the worktree's git config directly so `push.py`'s parent-dir resolution finds the values.
findings.finding: The persistent tickets-worktree push path relies on `push.py`'s `push_tickets_branch` resolving `tickets_branch()` and `tickets_remote()` from the PARENT of the tracker dir (`os.path.dirname(base_path)`). When `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR=/var/gerrit/site/reviewbot-tickets`, the parent is `/var/gerrit/site/` — a host bind-mount with no git repo or rebar config file — so `load_config(root='/var/gerrit/site/')` hits the `ConfigError` branch and `push_tickets_branch` silently returns without pushing. The plan's infra section does not address how the config (branch + remote) is made discoverable from this freestanding worktree path.
findings.location: Section D — Container paragraph; src/rebar/_store/push.py:103-155; src/rebar/config.py:128-145
findings.suggested_fix: In the Dockerfile entrypoint, after cloning the tickets worktree, run `git -C /var/gerrit/site/reviewbot-tickets/.tickets-tracker config rebar.sync.push always` and ensure a minimal `rebar.toml` is written to `/var/gerrit/site/` (or co-locate the worktree so its parent IS a valid rebar repo root). Alternatively, pre-set `REBAR_SYNC_REMOTE` and `REBAR_TRACKER_BRANCH` env vars in docker-compose.yml to bypass `load_config` entirely.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Anchor the `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH` grep to its required value: replace `grep -q 'REBAR_SYNC_PUSH'` with `grep -qE 'REBAR_SYNC_PUSH[[:space:]]*=[[:space:]]*always'` (or `grep -q 'REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=always'`) in the AC 4 proving command.
findings.finding: The AC 4 proving command `grep -q 'REBAR_SYNC_PUSH' infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` checks only for the presence of the string, not for the required value `=always`. A commented-out line, a disabled setting (`REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=never`), or an example in a comment would pass the grep while not satisfying the criterion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC 4 proving command: `grep -q 'REBAR_SYNC_PUSH' infra/compose/docker-compose.yml`
findings.suggested_fix: grep -q 'REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=always' infra/compose/docker-compose.yml

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the tickets worktree as an `external: true` named volume in `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` (e.g. `gerrit_reviewbot_tickets`) bound to `/var/gerrit/site/reviewbot-tickets` on the EBS data path, and add its creation to `compose-up.sh` alongside the other `gerrit_*` bind volumes — so the corpus worktree survives container recreation and `docker compose down -v`.
findings.finding: The new persistent tickets-worktree volume (`gerrit_reviewbot_tickets` or equivalent) is not mentioned in the docker-compose.yml `volumes:` block as `external: true`, and compose-up.sh's volume-creation step is not updated to create it — meaning `docker compose down -v` behaviour and EBS-bind wiring for the corpus worktree are unspecified.
findings.location: Section D (Infra) — `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` volumes block; `infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot`
findings.suggested_fix: In docker-compose.yml, add a `gerrit_reviewbot_tickets: { external: true }` volume entry and mount it into review-bot at `/var/gerrit/site/reviewbot-tickets`. In compose-up.sh, add the corresponding `docker volume create --driver local --opt type=none --opt o=bind --opt device=/var/gerrit/site/reviewbot-tickets gerrit_reviewbot_tickets` call (matching the existing gerrit_reviewbot pattern).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether trailer resolution operates on the tip commit's message only (already in scope) or truly all commits in the change; if the latter, name the Gerrit REST endpoint (e.g. `GET .../related` or `GET .../revisions`) and a new GerritClient method to fetch them, and add it to the Scope/files list.
findings.finding: The plan asserts trailer resolution runs `verify_commit.extract_ticket_refs(commit_message)` over ALL commits in the change, but the voter's `commit_message` variable holds only the tip revision's commit message (from `gc.get_commit(change_id, revision)`). Fetching commit messages for ALL commits in a multi-commit Gerrit change requires an additional Gerrit REST call (e.g. GET `.../related` or iterating the commit log) that the plan does not name and no existing GerritClient method provides.
findings.location: Section C — TRAILER RESOLUTION paragraph; src/rebar/review_bot/voter.py:364-368
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'over ALL commits in the change' with 'over the tip commit's message (the `commit_message` already in scope)' if single-commit coverage is sufficient. If genuine multi-commit coverage is needed, add `gc.get_related_commits(change_id, revision) -> list[str]` to GerritClient (calling `GET /a/changes/{id}/revisions/{rev}/related`) and enumerate those commit messages.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly that when the idempotency lookup finds an existing artifact, the sidecar emit (and push) are still unconditionally re-attempted ('upsert' semantics), and add a test case that exercises the found-but-sidecar-missing recovery path.
findings.finding: Partial-success state after a CREATE-succeeds / sidecar-emit-fails or CREATE-succeeds / push-fails sequence is unaddressed: on the next invocation of the same (change-id, revision), the idempotency lookup finds the artifact and skips CREATE, but the plan does not specify whether the sidecar emit is re-attempted on an existing artifact, leaving the artifact permanently sidecar-less with no diagnostic.
findings.location: Section C — idempotency paragraph; 'on any lookup failure the fallback is to CREATE a fresh artifact'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the idempotency logic to always call `sidecar.emit(...)` on the resolved artifact (whether just-created or pre-existing), making the emit step idempotent rather than the create-only step; the sidecar module should overwrite rather than append when the same (change_id, revision) key is present.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify idempotent worktree initialization: if the worktree directory already exists on the volume, run `git fetch && git reset --hard origin/tickets` (or equivalent) rather than re-cloning, so container restarts are safe without manual volume intervention.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how the one-time worktree materialization (clone of the tickets branch onto the persistent volume) behaves on container restart when the volume already contains a worktree — it may fail, leave a stale state, or require manual intervention.
findings.location: Section D (Infra): 'materialize that PERSISTENT tickets worktree once (clone of the `tickets` branch onto the mounted volume)'
findings.suggested_fix: In Dockerfile.reviewbot entrypoint: `if [ -d "$REBAR_TRACKER_DIR/.git" ]; then git -C "$REBAR_TRACKER_DIR" fetch origin && git -C "$REBAR_TRACKER_DIR" reset --hard origin/tickets; else git clone --branch tickets <url> "$REBAR_TRACKER_DIR"; fi`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's `plan_review/__init__.py` entry in the exemption-axis list: clarify that only the module docstring needs updating (no live `ticket_type` conditional exists there), and that the actual short-circuit edits are in `orchestrator.py` and `workflow_ops.py` only.
findings.finding: The plan names `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py` as a guard site for the `('bug','session_log')` short-circuit, but the actual exemption in that file is only in a docstring comment (`session_logs are exempt (PASS, runner=exempt)`) — the live short-circuit code lives in `orchestrator.py` and `workflow_ops.py`, not `__init__.py`. The `__init__.py` file itself has no `ticket_type` check or `('bug', 'session_log')` conditional branch to extend. Editing it for the `code_review` exemption axis is a hallucinated edit target for this specific file.
findings.location: Section A ('Gate exemption'); `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `__init__.py` from the parenthetical list of files with the `('bug','session_log')` short-circuit, or annotate it as 'docstring update only'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's `det_floor.py` entry in the exemption-axis list: clarify that only the comment at line 841 needs updating (no live `ticket_type` conditional exists there to modify).
findings.finding: The plan names `llm/plan_review/det_floor.py` as a guard site for the `('bug','session_log')` exemption axis, but a search of `det_floor.py` finds no `session_log` or `ticket_type` conditionals in executable code (only a comment at line 841 stating 'Bug/session_log are gate-exempt upstream, so they never…'). The live guard is upstream in `orchestrator.py`/`workflow_ops.py`; `det_floor.py` itself has no short-circuit tuple to extend.
findings.location: Section A ('Gate exemption'); `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/det_floor.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Annotate `det_floor.py` in the plan as 'comment update only, no live guard' to prevent implementor confusion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the tickets-branch git remote URL (or a placeholder env var like `$REBAR_TICKETS_REMOTE_URL` injected at deploy time) in Section D so the executor can write the Dockerfile entrypoint and URL-scoped credential helper config without stopping to ask.
findings.finding: Section D specifies that the Dockerfile entrypoint clones the `tickets` branch 'onto the mounted volume' and configures a URL-scoped credential helper using `credential.<tickets-url>.helper`, but the actual remote URL for the tickets worktree is never stated anywhere in the plan. An executor writing the Dockerfile entrypoint cannot determine the git remote URL to clone from or to scope the credential helper to without external information not in the plan.
findings.location: Section D (Infra), Implementation precision section ('git credential helper is URL-SCOPED via the `credential.<tickets-url>.helper` config key')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line to Section D such as: 'The remote URL is read from `$REBAR_TICKETS_REMOTE_URL` (a deploy-time env var injected alongside `REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT`); the credential helper is scoped to this URL via `git config credential.$REBAR_TICKETS_REMOTE_URL.helper ...` in the entrypoint script.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact credential helper form in Section D / Dockerfile.reviewbot — e.g., `git config --global credential.<url>.helper '!f() { printf "username=x-access-token\npassword=${REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT}\n"; }; f'` set in the container ENTRYPOINT script (reading the env var at runtime, not baked at build time) — so the 'token never lands on disk' claim can be independently verified.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the git credential helper is configured 'URL-SCOPED via the credential.<tickets-url>.helper config key' and that 'the token never lands on disk or in a remote URL,' but the current Dockerfile.reviewbot has zero credential-helper wiring and the plan describes the mechanism only abstractly ('Dockerfile entrypoint before the worktree clone'). The exact helper form (e.g., `git config credential.<url>.helper '!f() { echo password=$REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT; }; f'`) determines whether the PAT value is in an image layer or the env-expansion stays runtime-only — this is an unverified security-capability assertion.
findings.location: Section D (Infra) — 'URL-SCOPED (origin only, NOT global) git credential helper that echoes username=x-access-token / password=$REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT'; AC bullet 4 ('grep -q credential infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot'); Implementation precision final paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: In the Dockerfile.reviewbot plan, specify that the credential helper is set in a runtime entrypoint script (not a RUN layer), using `git config credential.<tickets-remote-url>.helper '!f() { printf "username=x-access-token\npassword=${REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT}\n"; }; f'` — the shell function reads the env var at credential-query time, never persisting the value to disk or the image layer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly in the plan whether `reviewbot-tickets-pat` is fetched with `get_param` (required, deploy-fail-fast) or `get_param_optional` (optional, silent-on-missing) in `fetch-secrets.sh`, and state the rationale.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether `REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT` is fetched as a required (`get_param`, fail-fast on missing/CHANGEME) or optional (`get_param_optional`) SSM parameter in `fetch-secrets.sh`. The existing script has two distinct patterns with very different failure modes: required params abort a deploy if unpopulated; optional params silently produce a blank env var, causing push auth to fail at runtime with no deploy-time signal.
findings.location: Section D — Materialization bullet; infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh:80-101
findings.suggested_fix: Use `get_param` (required) for `reviewbot-tickets-pat` — a missing PAT means the corpus push will silently fail on every review, which is a misconfiguration worth a loud deploy-time abort rather than silent runtime degradation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare `REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT` as a **fine-grained, single-repository PAT** (scope: `navapbc/rebar`, permission: `Contents: Read and write` only, targeting the `tickets` branch) in Section D and the operator runbook, consistent with every other PAT in this project (ADR-0011, g2p-ci-credentials.md).
findings.finding: The `REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT` PAT scope is undeclared on the boundary-crossing push path: the plan names it a 'RebarBotNava contents:write PAT' but never states whether it is a fine-grained, single-repository PAT or a classic OAuth PAT with `repo` scope. A classic PAT grants write access to ALL repositories owned by the account — far broader than the minimum needed (push to one branch of one repo). Every other credential in this project is explicitly fine-grained and single-repo-scoped (g2p-github-pat, replication deploy key, ADR-0011); the omission here leaves the access posture ambiguous on the only external trust boundary this plan crosses.
findings.location: Section D (Infra) — SSM slot description; AC4 infra proving command; 'RebarBotNava contents:write PAT' phrase
findings.suggested_fix: In Section D add: 'The operator MUST provision `reviewbot-tickets-pat` as a fine-grained GitHub PAT scoped to the `navapbc/rebar` repository only, with the `Contents: Read and write` permission, issued for the `RebarBotNava` machine account. Classic PATs MUST NOT be used.' Mirror the language in a new `infra/runbooks/reviewbot-tickets-pat.md` operator runbook referenced from the SSM slot comment in `ssm.tf`.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly call out in Section A that BOTH the source-endpoint check (line 175) AND the target-endpoint check (line 181) in `graph/_hierarchy.py` must be broadened to `ticket_type not in _NON_GRAPH_ARTIFACT_TYPES`, not just the generic 'its blocking-link refusal'.
findings.finding: The plan claims `graph/_hierarchy.py`'s blocking-link refusal 'reads the constant directly' (i.e., `_NON_GRAPH_ARTIFACT_TYPES`). But the actual code has TWO separate per-endpoint checks (`source_state.get('ticket_type') == 'session_log'` at line 175, and the same check for `target_state` at line 181). The plan's scope section only mentions one edit ('its blocking-link refusal reads the constant directly') without explicitly noting that BOTH the source-endpoint and target-endpoint checks need to be broadened. If only one check is updated, `code_review` tickets can still slip through as the other endpoint.
findings.location: Section A — 'graph/_hierarchy.py is edited EXPLICITLY (NOT via the flag): its _TYPE_TIER gains code_review: 0 and its blocking-link refusal reads the constant directly.'
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan, change 'its blocking-link refusal reads the constant directly' to 'its TWO blocking-link refusal checks (source endpoint at ~line 175 AND target endpoint at ~line 181) each become `ticket_type not in _NON_GRAPH_ARTIFACT_TYPES`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact predicate and error message for the `graph/_hierarchy.py` blocking-link refusal extension: define whether it becomes `ticket_type in _NON_GRAPH_ARTIFACT_TYPES` (a set membership test) and what error text to emit for a code_review endpoint.
findings.finding: The plan says `graph/_hierarchy.py`'s blocking-link refusal 'reads the constant directly' for code_review, but the existing refusal at lines 175-186 consists of two hardcoded `== 'session_log'` string comparisons with error messages that specifically say 'is a session_log'. The plan gives no protocol for what the error message should say for a code_review endpoint, nor does it specify the exact predicate change (membership test vs equality check).
findings.location: Section A ('Store/graph exclusion'); graph/_hierarchy.py:175-186
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the two single-type checks with: `if source_state.get('ticket_type') in _NON_GRAPH_ARTIFACT_TYPES: return {"error": f"ticket '{source_id}' is a {source_state.get('ticket_type')} (non-graph artifact) and cannot be a '{relation}' (blocking) link endpoint; use relates_to", ...}` — and specify this wording in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the semantic expansion of `include_session_logs` in `export_ndjson.py` and either rename/alias the flag (e.g. `include_artifact_types`) with a deprecation shim for the old name, or explicitly document that the flag's scope intentionally covers all non-graph artifact types going forward.
findings.finding: The `export_ndjson.py` change 'broadens the hardcoded `session_log` guard so default NDJSON exports exclude `code_review` too (opt in via `include_session_logs`)' silently expands the semantics of the existing `include_session_logs` opt-in flag to also return `code_review` artifacts. Existing callers that pass `include_session_logs=True` to retrieve session logs will now receive unexpected `code_review` records without any acknowledgement, migration note, or flag-rename.
findings.location: Section A ('Export: broaden the hardcoded `session_log` guard in `_io/export_ndjson.py`') and AC1 ('hidden from default `list` but returned by `search`')
findings.suggested_fix: Rename the flag to `include_non_graph_artifacts` (or `include_artifact_logs`) and add a backward-compat alias `include_session_logs=include_non_graph_artifacts` with a deprecation log line; note this in the plan's compat section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least a simple retry (e.g., up to 3 attempts with brief backoff) for the git push step, and/or a WAL/pending-push queue so locally-committed artifacts are re-attempted on the next reviewer cycle; document the recovery path explicitly in Section D.
findings.finding: The git-push failure path has no retry or backoff: a transient network error silently drops the artifact with only a log line, and the plan specifies no mechanism to recover or re-push a locally-committed-but-not-pushed artifact on the next review of the same change.
findings.location: Section C / Section D — 'All emit/link/push failures are caught and logged; they NEVER fail the review vote'
findings.suggested_fix: After `store.record_vote`, wrap the push in a retry loop (3 attempts, exponential backoff starting at 1 s); on exhaustion log at ERROR and append the artifact id to a `pending-push.log` file in the worktree that the next reviewbot startup drains before beginning its first review.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one structured log field or lightweight counter (e.g., `emit_status=success|create_failed|sidecar_failed|push_failed`) per artifact attempt so operators can alert on error rates without parsing free-text logs.
findings.finding: No metric or alert is defined for failures in the artifact-emit pipeline (CREATE failures, sidecar-emit failures, push failures); the only observability is log lines, meaning operators cannot build a dashboard or alert on artifact-corpus health without scraping unstructured logs.
findings.location: Section C — log format paragraph; Section D
findings.suggested_fix: Emit a single structured log line at the end of the emit path with fields `change_id`, `revision`, `artifact_id`, `emit_status` (one of: `success`, `create_failed`, `sidecar_failed`, `push_failed`, `push_retry_exhausted`) — this enables log-based alerting with a simple filter without adding a new metrics dependency.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract the artifact title format into a named constant (e.g., `CODE_REVIEW_TITLE_FMT`) shared between the idempotency lookup and the CREATE call, and consider using `change_fingerprint` as the primary idempotency key stored in a dedicated metadata field rather than relying on the human-readable title string.
findings.finding: The artifact title used as the idempotency key (`f'code-review: {change_id} @ {revision}'`) is a hardcoded business rule embedded in the emit path; if the title format ever changes (e.g., to include a repo prefix or shorten the revision), pre-existing artifacts become invisible to the idempotency lookup, silently creating duplicates rather than updating.
findings.location: Implementation precision section — 'Idempotency lookup uses … title field == f"code-review: {change_id} @ {revision}"'
findings.suggested_fix: Define `CODE_REVIEW_TITLE_FMT = 'code-review: {change_id} @ {revision}'` in `code_review/sidecar.py` and import it in `voter.py`; additionally store `change_fingerprint` in the artifact's metadata/tags so a future lookup can use the stable hash as a fallback.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a runtime kill-switch (e.g., `REBAR_CODE_REVIEW_ARTIFACT=0` env var) that disables artifact emit/push without requiring a container redeploy, so the emit path can be disabled independently if it causes issues post-deploy.
findings.finding: The new emit path (artifact create + trailer link + git push) activates for 100% of reviews on the first deploy with no feature flag, env-variable kill-switch, or canary ramp. If any part of the create/link/push path causes unexpected latency or errors that leak past the catch block, every review is affected simultaneously.
findings.location: Section C (Reviewbot emit); Section D (Infra); AC bullet 3
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the entire artifact-emit block in voter.py with `if os.getenv('REBAR_CODE_REVIEW_ARTIFACT', '1') != '0':` so the path can be toggled via compose env without a code redeploy.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document an explicit rollback procedure: at minimum, specify how old container images handle unknown ticket types encountered in the store (graceful skip expected?), and note that the tickets branch does NOT need cleanup on rollback (code_review tickets are harmless to old readers if unknown-type tolerance is confirmed).
findings.finding: No explicit rollback procedure is described for the deployed change. The plan adds a new ticket type to a shared git store; if the schema change or emit code causes issues requiring rollback, the operator has no documented steps — particularly since the tickets branch already has pushed code_review artifacts that older code may not handle gracefully.
findings.location: Section D (Infra); Section A (type guard sites); AC bullet 1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit unit-test cases to `tests/unit/test_code_review_artifact.py` for (a) emit/link/push failure → exception caught, vote result unaffected; and (b) duplicate submit of the same (change-id, revision) → existing artifact reused, no second ticket created.
findings.finding: The test plan specifies no failure-path tests for the emit step itself: store errors, link failures, or push failures during artifact creation are described as non-fatal and caught/logged, but no unit test verifies this contract (the vote must succeed even when the artifact emit throws). Idempotency — same (change-id, revision) submitted twice producing the same artifact rather than a duplicate — is also a specified behavior with no test case named.
findings.location: AC#3 / Section C / `tests/unit/test_code_review_artifact.py`
findings.suggested_fix: In the fake-store test fixture, add a case where `rebar.create_ticket` raises; assert the voter still returns its decision dict and that the exception is only logged. Add a second call to the emit helper with the same change-id+revision and assert `rebar.create_ticket` is called only once (idempotency).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `change_fingerprint` should route through `rebar._store.canonical.content_hash` (the designated single seam for sorted-key JSON sha256, per its own docstring) rather than re-implementing `json.dumps(basis, sort_keys=True) → sha256 → [:16]` inline.
findings.finding: `change_fingerprint` proposes a new function doing `sha256(sorted-key JSON basis)[:16]` but the codebase already has `rebar._store.canonical.content_hash(doc)` as an explicit designated single seam for sorted-key compact JSON sha256. The plan neither uses nor mentions this existing canonical primitive — it instead mirrors the same inline pattern used by `material_fingerprint` in `pass1.py`, which is itself an older pre-seam inline that the canonical module was created to consolidate.
findings.location: Section B — 'change_fingerprint(change_id, revision, changed_files, diff_text)'; src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py (new function); src/rebar/_store/canonical.py:74 (content_hash)
findings.suggested_fix: In `change_fingerprint`, build the basis dict and call `from rebar._store.canonical import content_hash; return content_hash(basis)[:16]` instead of inlining the sha256/json.dumps pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_io/_cli.py` to the plan's scope list (or explicitly acknowledge that `--include-session-logs` will also include `code_review` after the guard broadening) so the behavioral change to the existing CLI flag is intentional and documented.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_io/_cli.py` is an unenumerated consumer of `export_ndjson.py`'s `include_session_logs` flag. When the plan broadens the `export_ndjson.py` guard from `ttype == 'session_log'` to `ttype not in _NON_GRAPH_ARTIFACT_TYPES`, the existing `--include-session-logs` CLI flag (at `_io/_cli.py:63`, `include_session_logs = True`) will silently begin including `code_review` artifacts too. This file is absent from the plan's scope list.
findings.location: Section A ('Export') and Scope / files list; `src/rebar/_io/_cli.py:63`
findings.suggested_fix: Either add `src/rebar/_io/_cli.py` to the Scope / files section with a note that `--include-session-logs` intentionally covers both artifact types, or introduce a separate `--include-artifacts` flag and update `_cli.py` accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the plan intentionally leaves `recent_session_logs_state` (`reads.py:471`) without `exclude_session_logs=True`, or add a note that it will load all `code_review` tickets before filtering them out (performance consideration at corpus scale).
findings.finding: The plan's `reads.py` list-visibility fix uses `exclude_session_logs=(ticket_type not in _NON_GRAPH_ARTIFACT_TYPES)`, but `list --type=code_review` requires the predicate to evaluate to `False` (i.e., do NOT exclude) only when the type filter is exactly `'code_review'`. The current code is `(ticket_type != 'session_log')`, which means `list` with no type filter excludes `session_log` but the plan's broadened predicate `ticket_type not in _NON_GRAPH_ARTIFACT_TYPES` means `list` with no type filter will exclude BOTH. However `list --type=code_review` will pass `ticket_type='code_review'` and the predicate becomes `'code_review' not in {'session_log','code_review'}` = `False` — correct. BUT `list` with no type filter AND no `--type` arg passes `ticket_type=None` or `''`, making `None not in _NON_GRAPH_ARTIFACT_TYPES` = `True` (exclude both). This is the intended behavior per the plan. The plan's precision here is correct. However, the plan's `reads.py` fix does not address `recent_session_logs_state` (line 471–472), which calls `reduce_all_tickets` without `exclude_session_logs` and then manually filters `ticket_type == 'session_log'`. After the change, calling `reduce_all_tickets` without `exclude_session_logs=True` in `recent_session_logs_state` will include `code_review` tickets in the full set, and then the `== 'session_log'` post-filter correctly excludes them from the result. This is fine functionally but is an unenumerated site.
findings.location: Section A ('Visibility'); `src/rebar/_engine_support/reads.py:465-475` (`recent_session_logs_state`)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a CloudWatch metric emission (e.g. `rebar/host:artifact_emit_errors`) for code_review artifact create/push failures in the reviewbot's observability probe (or via a structured log pattern) and wire a corresponding alarm so persistent emit failures page the operator.
findings.finding: Artifact-emit failures (create ticket, emit sidecar, push) are explicitly non-fatal and caught/logged, but no CloudWatch alarm or metric is wired for persistent emit failures — a broken emit path produces silent corpus gaps with no operator alert.
findings.location: Section C — emit/link/push failure handling; Section D — observability; AC #3 (non-fatal logging)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate ALL hardcoded `'session_log'` string comparisons across `reducer/_api.py`, `reads.py` (both the `list_states` predicate AND `recent_session_logs_state`), `export_ndjson.py`, and `graph/` that need explicit edits — the plan's 'callers inherit with NO edit' claim is incorrect for these sites; add them to the Scope/files list and specify the exact predicate change at each.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new shared constant `_NON_GRAPH_ARTIFACT_TYPES` in `reducer/_api.py` and asserts all callers 'transparently inherit' the broadened exclusion — but several call sites hardcode the string `'session_log'` directly and will NOT pick up `code_review` exclusion without explicit edits. Confirmed hardcoded sites: `reads.py:373` (`ticket_type != 'session_log'`), `reads.py:472` (`ticket_type == 'session_log'` in `recent_session_logs_state`), `export_ndjson.py:103` (`ttype == 'session_log'`), `graph/_unblock.py:128-131` (passes `exclude_session_logs=True` but the `reduce_all_tickets` call is only as broad as the predicate allows), `graph/_graph.py:76-77` (inline comment + path). The plan lists the `reads.py` visibility site but only the `exclude_session_logs=` predicate change — it does not address the inner `recent_session_logs_state` filter that hardcodes `session_log`.
findings.location: Section A ('Store/graph exclusion'); reducer/_api.py; reads.py:373,472; export_ndjson.py:103; graph/_unblock.py:128-131; graph/_graph.py:76-77
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, explicitly list `reads.py:recent_session_logs_state` (add `ticket_type != 'code_review'` filter or make it `_NON_SESSION_ARTIFACT_TYPES`-aware) and `reducer/_api.py:244` (change the hardcoded string to `r.get('ticket_type') not in _NON_GRAPH_ARTIFACT_TYPES`). Acknowledge these are direct edits, not transparent inheritance.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `export_ndjson.py`'s `include_session_logs` parameter is renamed (e.g., `include_artifact_logs`) or a separate `include_code_reviews` parameter is added, so the two artifact types can be opted into independently.
findings.finding: The plan says `export_ndjson.py` should be broadened so 'default NDJSON exports exclude `code_review` too (opt in via `include_session_logs`)'. The existing parameter is named `include_session_logs` — the plan's reuse of this parameter name for code_review exclusion opt-in means callers that pass `include_session_logs=True` (e.g., `_io/_cli.py:63`) would unexpectedly also include code_review artifacts, conflating two distinct opt-ins. The plan does not specify a new parameter name, a renamed parameter, or separate boolean flags.
findings.location: Section A ('Export'); export_ndjson.py:76; _io/_cli.py:63
findings.suggested_fix: Add a separate `include_code_reviews: bool = False` parameter alongside `include_session_logs` in `iter_export_states` / `export_tickets`, mirroring the existing opt-in pattern rather than collapsing both under one flag.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief ADR or `CLAUDE.md` section documenting (a) the 'bulk artifact type' pattern (mirror session_log exemptions, out of dependency graph, hidden from list), (b) the join-key contract (norm_id reuse, change_fingerprint construction), and (c) the reviewbot-as-store-writer decision with its credential/worktree setup.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a significant new architectural decision — the reviewbot becomes a first-class writer to the shared tickets store, a new artifact type is added to the type system, and a new join-key pattern (change_fingerprint / norm_id reuse across subsystems) is established — but no ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or design-doc update is planned to capture this decision and its rationale for future maintainers.
findings.location: Section A ('Why a new ticket TYPE') and Section B ('Join keys'); Scope / files list

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `_NON_GRAPH_ARTIFACT_TYPES` has at least 3 direct consuming import sites (not just flag-passthrough inheritors) or consider inlining `ticket_type not in {"session_log", "code_review"}` at the two sites rather than introducing a new shared cross-package constant.
findings.finding: `_NON_GRAPH_ARTIFACT_TYPES` is a new named module constant with only 2 direct import/consumption sites as specified by the plan (`reducer/_api.py` and `_engine_support/reads.py`). The graph callers (`_graph.py`, `_blockers.py`, `_unblock.py`, `_ready.py`, `next_batch.py`) pass the existing boolean `exclude_session_logs=True` and never import the constant — so the constant's direct call-site count is 2, below the Rule-of-Three threshold for justifying a shared named abstraction.
findings.location: Section A — 'broaden the single reducer predicate … a new shared module constant'; Implementation precision — 'predicate broadens to ticket_type not in _NON_GRAPH_ARTIFACT_TYPES, a new module constant'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `src/rebar/schemas/gen_types.py` (or equivalent) exists and is runnable as `python -m rebar.schemas.gen_types` before relying on it as the types.py regeneration command.
findings.finding: The plan states the ticket type enum source-of-truth is `common.schema.json` and that `types.py` is regenerated via `python -m rebar.schemas.gen_types` (do NOT hand-edit). But `src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json` currently has `ticket_type` enum as `["bug","epic","story","task","session_log"]` with NO `code_review` — this is expected (it's new work). However, the plan does not name or verify that `rebar.schemas.gen_types` exists as a runnable module. If that regeneration command does not exist or has a different invocation, `types.py` won't be regenerated properly.
findings.location: Section A — 'regenerate `src/rebar/types.py` via `python -m rebar.schemas.gen_types` (do NOT hand-edit)'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
